message:
fix(btree): replace cached BeTree len with computed count to prevent drift

The BeTree struct maintained a cached `len` field that was incremented
on insert and decremented on delete. However, this bookkeeping was
unreliable because:

1. The BeTree uses buffered (write-optimized) messaging: Insert and
   Delete messages are buffered at the root and lazily flushed down
   the tree. At the time a message is applied, the tree cannot cheaply
   determine whether the key already exists without performing a full
   get() traversal. The previous code did call self.get() before every
   apply_message(), but this defeated the purpose of write buffering
   and was still subtly incorrect when multiple buffered messages for
   the same key were pending.

2. Sequences like Insert(k) -> Delete(k) -> Insert(k) could cause the
   counter to drift if intermediate flushes changed which messages were
   resolved.

The fix removes the `len` field entirely and computes len() on demand
by collecting all pending messages via collect_all() and counting keys
with a Some (non-deleted) value. This is O(n) but len() is not called
in hot paths -- it is only used in Debug formatting and explicit
queries. The is_empty() method now delegates to len() for consistency.

This also removes the pre-lookup of keys and the len-tracking logic
from apply_message(), making inserts and deletes cheaper since they no
longer need a full tree traversal before buffering the message.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 <noreply@anthropic.com>
